modules.title: Java Core & OOP Foundations

sections.heading: Why Java Still Dominates
sections.content: Java powers 90% of Indian IT service companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, HCL). It runs on 3 billion devices worldwide. Android apps, enterprise backends, banking systems, and government portals — all Java. Learning Java is not optional for Indian IT job seekers. The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) makes Java platform-independent — write once, run anywhere. Java is statically typed (you declare variable types), compiled (javac converts to bytecode), and garbage-collected (no manual memory management like C++). Java 8 introduced lambdas and streams which revolutionized how Java code is written. Java 17 is the current LTS (Long Term Support) version used in production.
sections.tip: In interviews, know the 8 primitive types: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean. String is NOT a primitive — it is a class.

sections.heading: OOP — The Four Pillars
sections.content: Object-Oriented Programming is the foundation of Java. Every interview tests these four concepts. Understanding them deeply separates a junior from a senior developer. Encapsulation bundles data and methods together, hiding internal state. Inheritance allows classes to inherit behavior from parent classes. Polymorphism means one interface, multiple implementations. Abstraction hides complexity and shows only essential features.
sections.tip: The interviewer will ask: What is polymorphism? Answer: Same method name behaves differently based on the actual object type. The @Override annotation is not required but is best practice — compiler checks you are actually overriding a parent method.

sections.heading: Abstract Classes vs Interfaces
sections.content: Abstract classes provide partial implementation — some methods implemented, some left abstract for subclasses. Interfaces define a contract — what methods a class MUST implement. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default methods with implementation. This is one of the most commonly asked interview questions: When to use abstract class vs interface?
sections.tip: Interview answer: Use abstract class when classes share common state/behavior. Use interface when unrelated classes need the same capability. Example: Dog and Cat share Animal (abstract class). Both Dog and Serializable are unrelated — use interface.

sections.heading: Access Modifiers
sections.content: Java has four access levels that control visibility. Understanding these is critical for encapsulation and is tested in every interview.
sections.tip: Make everything private by default. Only make public what needs to be accessed outside. This is encapsulation in practice.

key_points: Java has 8 primitives: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean
String is a class, not a primitive
Four OOP pillars: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction
Abstract class = partial implementation, Interface = contract
A class extends ONE class but implements MANY interfaces

module_quiz.question: String is a primitive type in Java?
module_quiz.explanation: String is a class in java.lang package. The 8 primitives are byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean.

module_quiz.question: final keyword on variable means?
module_quiz.explanation: final makes a variable constant. Once assigned, its value cannot change.

module_quiz.question: A class can extend how many classes?
module_quiz.explanation: Java supports single inheritance for classes. A class can extend exactly one class but implement many interfaces.

module_quiz.question: @Override annotation does?
options: Tells compiler to verify method overrides parent
module_quiz.explanation: @Override is optional but best practice. Compiler verifies the method actually exists in parent class.

module_quiz.question: Most restrictive access modifier?
module_quiz.explanation: private is most restrictive — accessible only within the same class. Order: private > default > protected > public.

modules.title: Collections Framework

sections.heading: List — ArrayList vs LinkedList
sections.content: List is an ordered collection that allows duplicates. ArrayList is backed by a dynamic array — fast random access O(1) but slow insertion/deletion in middle O(N). LinkedList is backed by doubly-linked nodes — fast insertion/deletion O(1) but slow random access O(N). In 95% of cases, use ArrayList. LinkedList is rarely better in practice due to cache locality.
sections.tip: ArrayList for 95% of cases. Only use LinkedList when you need frequent add/remove from both ends (queue pattern). Interview question: ArrayList vs LinkedList time complexity for get, add, remove.

sections.heading: Set — HashSet, LinkedHashSet, TreeSet
sections.content: Set stores unique elements — no duplicates allowed. HashSet is fastest (O(1) operations) but unordered. LinkedHashSet maintains insertion order. TreeSet keeps elements sorted but slower (O(log N)). Choosing the right Set depends on whether you need ordering.
sections.tip: To remove duplicates from a List, convert to HashSet and back. This is the most common interview pattern for deduplication.

sections.heading: Map — HashMap, LinkedHashMap, TreeMap
sections.content: Map stores key-value pairs. HashMap is the most used data structure in Java interviews. O(1) get/put average. Keys must be unique. Values can duplicate. Understanding how HashMap works internally (hashing, buckets, collision handling) is asked in every senior interview.
sections.tip: HashMap getOrDefault() and computeIfAbsent() are the two methods that make code clean. Every senior developer uses them. Character frequency counting with HashMap is asked in 80% of Java coding interviews.

sections.heading: Queue and Stack
sections.content: Queue is FIFO (First In First Out). Stack is LIFO (Last In First Out). In modern Java, use Deque interface for both.
sections.tip: Never use java.util.Stack — it is legacy and synchronized (slow). Use ArrayDeque as stack (push/pop) and LinkedList as queue (offer/poll). PriorityQueue is essential for top-K problems in interviews.

key_points: ArrayList for 95% of list needs — O(1) random access
HashSet for uniqueness — O(1) contains check
HashMap for key-value — getOrDefault and computeIfAbsent are essential
Use ArrayDeque as Stack, LinkedList as Queue
PriorityQueue for min/max heap problems

module_quiz.question: ArrayList.get(5) time complexity?
module_quiz.explanation: ArrayList is backed by array. Direct index access is O(1). LinkedList.get(5) would be O(N).

module_quiz.question: How to remove duplicates from List?
options: Convert to HashSet then back to List
module_quiz.explanation: new ArrayList<>(new HashSet<>(list)) removes duplicates because Set doesn't allow them.

module_quiz.question: HashMap.getOrDefault('key', 0) does?
options: Returns value if key exists, otherwise returns 0
module_quiz.explanation: Returns the value for the key, or the default value (0) if key is not found. Prevents NullPointerException.

module_quiz.question: Which Stack class to use in modern Java?
module_quiz.explanation: java.util.Stack is legacy and synchronized. ArrayDeque is faster and recommended for stack operations.

module_quiz.question: TreeSet stores elements in?
module_quiz.explanation: TreeSet uses a Red-Black tree internally, keeping elements in natural sorted order (or custom Comparator).

modules.title: Exception Handling & Strings

sections.heading: Exception Handling
sections.content: Java has checked exceptions (must handle) and unchecked exceptions (runtime). Try-catch-finally is the basic pattern. Custom exceptions make code more expressive. Resource management with try-with-resources prevents leaks.
sections.tip: Checked exceptions must be caught or declared with throws. Unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException subclasses) dont need explicit handling. Interview question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?

sections.heading: String Handling
sections.content: Strings in Java are immutable — every modification creates a new String object. For concatenation in loops, use StringBuilder (not +). String pool is a special memory area where Java caches string literals.
sections.tip: String + in a loop creates new objects each iteration — O(N squared). Always use StringBuilder in loops. This is a classic interview performance question. And NEVER compare strings with == — always use .equals().

key_points: Checked exceptions must be handled, unchecked are optional
Try-with-resources auto-closes files and connections
Strings are immutable — every operation creates new String
Use StringBuilder for concatenation in loops
Compare strings with .equals() NEVER with ==

module_quiz.question: s1 == s2 for strings checks?
options: Reference equality — same object in memory
module_quiz.explanation: == checks if both variables point to same object. .equals() checks content. Always use .equals() for strings.

module_quiz.question: Checked exception must be?
options: Caught with try-catch or declared with throws
module_quiz.explanation: Checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException) must be explicitly handled or declared.

module_quiz.question: StringBuilder vs String for loops?
module_quiz.explanation: String creates new object each concatenation O(N squared). StringBuilder modifies in place O(N).

module_quiz.question: try-with-resources does?
options: Auto-closes resources like files and connections
module_quiz.explanation: Resources implementing AutoCloseable are automatically closed when try block ends.

module_quiz.question: String pool is?
options: Memory area caching string literals for reuse
module_quiz.explanation: Java caches string literals in a special pool. Same literal string reuses the same object.

modules.title: Java 8+ Features

sections.heading: Lambda Expressions
sections.content: Lambdas are anonymous functions introduced in Java 8. They make code concise by replacing verbose anonymous inner classes. A lambda can be used wherever a functional interface (interface with one abstract method) is expected.
sections.tip: Method references (String::compareTo, System.out::println) are shorthand for lambdas when you are just calling an existing method. Know the four types: static (Math::max), instance (String::length), object (System.out::println), constructor (ArrayList::new).

sections.heading: Streams API
sections.content: Streams process collections in a functional pipeline: source → intermediate operations → terminal operation. They dont modify the original collection. Streams are lazy — intermediate operations dont execute until a terminal operation is called. This is the most important Java 8 feature for interviews.
sections.tip: The stream pipeline is: source.stream().intermediate().intermediate().terminal(). Intermediate: filter, map, sorted, distinct, limit, skip. Terminal: collect, count, forEach, reduce, min, max, findFirst, anyMatch. Collectors.groupingBy is the most powerful — equivalent to SQL GROUP BY.

sections.heading: Optional
sections.content: Optional is a container that may or may not contain a value. It prevents NullPointerException by forcing you to handle the absence of a value explicitly. Introduced in Java 8 as a better alternative to null checks.
sections.tip: Never call .get() without checking isPresent() — it throws NoSuchElementException. Always use orElse(), orElseGet(), or ifPresent(). Optional forces you to handle missing values explicitly.

key_points: Lambdas replace verbose anonymous classes
Method references are shorthand for simple lambdas
Streams: filter → map → collect is the core pattern
Collectors.groupingBy is SQL GROUP BY equivalent
Optional prevents NullPointerException

module_quiz.question: Lambda (a, b) -> a + b replaces?
options: Anonymous inner class with one method
module_quiz.explanation: Lambdas provide concise syntax for implementing functional interfaces (one abstract method).

module_quiz.question: stream().filter().map().collect() — filter and map are?
module_quiz.explanation: Intermediate operations are lazy. They dont execute until a terminal operation like collect() is called.

module_quiz.question: Collectors.groupingBy does?
options: Groups elements by a key like SQL GROUP BY
module_quiz.explanation: Creates a Map where key is the group and value is list of elements in that group.

module_quiz.question: Optional.orElse('default') does?
options: Returns value if present, otherwise default
module_quiz.explanation: Returns the contained value if present, otherwise returns the provided default value.

module_quiz.question: Method reference String::length is same as?
module_quiz.explanation: Method reference is shorthand for a lambda that just calls an existing method.

modules.title: Multithreading & Concurrency

sections.heading: Creating Threads
sections.content: Java supports multithreading natively. Two ways to create threads: extend Thread class or implement Runnable interface. Modern Java prefers ExecutorService for thread management.
sections.tip: Never use raw Thread in production. Always use ExecutorService. Runnable returns void, Callable returns a value. This distinction is asked in every thread interview.

sections.heading: Synchronization
sections.content: When multiple threads access shared data, race conditions occur. Synchronized blocks ensure only one thread accesses critical section at a time. Modern Java provides ConcurrentHashMap, AtomicInteger, and other thread-safe utilities.
sections.tip: count++ is NOT atomic — it is read, modify, write in 3 steps. Two threads can read same value and both write same result. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() is the simplest fix. ConcurrentHashMap is the production choice for thread-safe maps.

key_points: Runnable returns void, Callable returns value
ExecutorService for production thread management
synchronized prevents race conditions
AtomicInteger for thread-safe counters without locks
ConcurrentHashMap for thread-safe Map

module_quiz.question: Runnable vs Callable?
module_quiz.explanation: Runnable.run() returns void. Callable.call() returns a value and can throw exceptions.

module_quiz.question: count++ is thread-safe?
module_quiz.explanation: Two threads can read same value, both increment, and write same result — losing one increment.

module_quiz.question: ExecutorService over raw Thread because?
options: Manages thread pool, reuses threads, prevents resource exhaustion
module_quiz.explanation: Thread pools reuse threads instead of creating new ones. Manages lifecycle and prevents too many threads.

module_quiz.question: ConcurrentHashMap vs Collections.synchronizedMap?
options: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads, better performance
module_quiz.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap uses segment locking — multiple threads can read/write different segments simultaneously.

module_quiz.question: Future.get() does?
options: Blocks until the async result is available
module_quiz.explanation: get() blocks the calling thread until the Callable completes and returns its result.

modules.title: Interview Questions & Answers

sections.heading: equals() vs == vs hashCode()
sections.content: The most fundamental Java interview question. == compares references. equals() compares content. hashCode() must be consistent with equals() for HashMap to work correctly.
sections.tip: If you override equals() you MUST override hashCode(). This is the #1 Java rule. Without it, HashMap keys break because equal objects hash to different buckets.

sections.heading: Immutable Class
sections.content: Creating an immutable class is a common interview question. All fields final, no setters, defensive copies.
sections.tip: For immutable classes: make class final, all fields private final, no setters, defensive copy mutable objects in constructor and getter. Java Records (14+) do this automatically.

sections.heading: Singleton Pattern
sections.content: Ensure only one instance of a class exists. Multiple ways to implement.
sections.tip: Enum singleton is the best — handles serialization, reflection, and thread-safety automatically. Joshua Bloch recommends it in Effective Java.

key_points: Override equals AND hashCode together — always
== is reference comparison, equals is content comparison
Immutable: final class, private final fields, no setters, defensive copies
Records (Java 14+) create immutable classes automatically
Enum singleton is the simplest and safest pattern

module_quiz.question: If equals() overridden, must also override?
module_quiz.explanation: hashCode contract requires: equal objects must have equal hash codes. Without this, HashMap breaks.

module_quiz.question: Immutable class requires?
options: Final class + private final fields + no setters + defensive copies
module_quiz.explanation: All four are needed. Defensive copies prevent external modification of mutable fields.

module_quiz.question: Java Record is?
options: Immutable data class with auto-generated methods
module_quiz.explanation: Records auto-generate constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, and toString for immutable data.

module_quiz.question: Best Singleton in Java?
module_quiz.explanation: Enum singleton handles serialization, reflection attacks, and thread-safety automatically.

module_quiz.question: new String('Hi') == 'Hi'?
module_quiz.explanation: new String() creates object on heap. Literal 'Hi' is in String pool. Different memory locations.

modules.title: Generics & Functional Interfaces

sections.heading: Generics
sections.content: Generics provide compile-time type safety. They let you write code that works with any type while catching type errors at compile time instead of runtime.
sections.tip: PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. If you read from a collection, use extends. If you write to it, use super. This is asked in senior Java interviews.

sections.heading: Functional Interfaces Deep Dive
sections.content: Java 8 provides built-in functional interfaces in java.util.function package. These are the building blocks for lambdas and streams.
sections.tip: Know these four: Predicate (test), Function (transform), Consumer (use), Supplier (create). They appear everywhere in Streams, Optional, and CompletableFuture.

key_points: Generics catch type errors at compile time
PECS: Producer Extends Consumer Super
Predicate=test Function=transform Consumer=use Supplier=create
Functional interfaces have exactly one abstract method
Combine predicates with and() or() negate()

module_quiz.question: Box<int> is valid?
options: No — must use Box<Integer> wrapper class
module_quiz.explanation: Generics dont support primitives. Use wrapper classes: Integer, Double, Boolean, Character, Long.

module_quiz.question: PECS stands for?
module_quiz.explanation: Read from collection = extends (producer). Write to collection = super (consumer).

module_quiz.question: Predicate<T> returns?
module_quiz.explanation: Predicate.test(T) returns boolean. Used for filtering in streams.

module_quiz.question: Function<String, Integer> means?
module_quiz.explanation: Function<T,R> takes T (String) as input and returns R (Integer).

module_quiz.question: Supplier<T> takes?
options: Nothing — takes no arguments returns T
module_quiz.explanation: Supplier has no input parameters. get() returns a value of type T. Used for lazy object creation.

modules.title: Design Patterns in Java

sections.heading: Factory and Builder Patterns
sections.content: Factory creates objects without exposing creation logic. Builder handles complex object construction with many optional parameters.
sections.tip: Builder is used in every Java framework: StringBuilder, Stream.Builder, HttpClient.newBuilder(), Retrofit. If constructor has more than 3-4 parameters, use Builder.

sections.heading: Observer and Strategy Patterns
sections.content: Observer notifies multiple subscribers when state changes. Strategy allows swapping algorithms at runtime.
sections.tip: Strategy pattern with Java lambdas becomes very clean: service.setStrategy(amount -> processUPI(amount)). No need for separate classes.

key_points: Factory centralizes object creation logic
Builder handles complex construction with optional parameters
Observer for event-driven notification systems
Strategy for swappable algorithms at runtime
All patterns use interfaces for flexibility

module_quiz.question: Factory pattern advantage?
options: Hides creation logic caller doesnt know exact class
module_quiz.explanation: Caller asks for 'email notification' without knowing EmailNotification class exists.

module_quiz.question: Builder pattern for?
module_quiz.explanation: Builder provides readable construction when class has many optional fields.

module_quiz.question: Observer pattern is?
options: Multiple subscribers notified when state changes
module_quiz.explanation: Publishers emit events, subscribers react. Used in GUI, message queues, reactive programming.

module_quiz.question: Strategy pattern allows?
module_quiz.explanation: Different strategies implement same interface. Context switches between them dynamically.

module_quiz.question: Builder returns this in setters for?
module_quiz.explanation: Returning this allows fluent API where methods chain together readably.

modules.title: JVM Internals & Memory

sections.heading: JVM Memory Model
sections.content: Understanding JVM memory is essential for debugging OutOfMemoryError and optimizing performance. The JVM divides memory into Stack (method calls, local variables) and Heap (objects). Heap is further divided into Young Generation and Old Generation for garbage collection efficiency.
sections.tip: Stack is per-thread and fast. Heap is shared and slower. Primitives go on stack, objects go on heap. String literals go to String Pool. This memory model is asked in every senior Java interview.

sections.heading: Garbage Collection Deep Dive
sections.content: GC automatically frees memory from unreachable objects. Understanding GC helps debug performance issues and memory leaks.
sections.tip: System.gc() is just a suggestion — JVM can ignore it. Never rely on it in production. Static inner classes dont hold outer reference — use them to prevent memory leaks.

key_points: Stack = per thread method calls and primitives
Heap = shared all objects live here
Young Gen for new objects Old Gen for long-lived
G1GC is default since Java 9
Static inner class prevents outer class memory leak

module_quiz.question: Local int x = 5 stored where?
module_quiz.explanation: Primitives and local variables are stored on the stack. Objects are on the heap.

module_quiz.question: new Employee() object stored where?
module_quiz.explanation: Objects created with new are always stored on the heap. The reference is on the stack.

module_quiz.question: System.gc() guarantees collection?
options: No — its just a suggestion to JVM
module_quiz.explanation: JVM may ignore gc() request. Garbage collection timing is JVMs decision.

module_quiz.question: Memory leak in Java happens when?
options: Objects referenced but never used pile up
module_quiz.explanation: Java has GC but leaks happen when objects are still referenced (static collections, listeners) even though theyre not needed.

module_quiz.question: Static inner class vs inner class?
options: Static doesnt hold reference to outer class preventing leaks
module_quiz.explanation: Non-static inner class implicitly references outer instance. Static inner class is independent.

modules.title: Spring Boot & Production Java

sections.heading: Spring Boot Essentials
sections.content: Spring Boot is the most used Java framework for building production applications. 90% of Java job postings require Spring knowledge. It handles dependency injection, web servers, database access, and configuration automatically.
sections.tip: Spring Boot interview essentials: @RestController for APIs, @Service for business logic, @Repository for database, @Autowired for dependency injection. Know the Controller → Service → Repository layered architecture.

sections.heading: SOLID Principles in Java
sections.content: SOLID makes code maintainable when many developers work on the same codebase. Every senior Java interview covers these principles.
sections.tip: Dependency Inversion is the most important SOLID principle. Spring entire framework is built on it. @Autowired injects dependencies through interfaces, not concrete classes.

key_points: Spring Boot: Controller → Service → Repository architecture
@Autowired for dependency injection
JpaRepository provides CRUD without writing SQL
SOLID principles for maintainable code
Dependency Inversion = depend on interfaces not implementations

module_quiz.question: @RestController combines?
module_quiz.explanation: @RestController = @Controller (handle requests) + @ResponseBody (return JSON directly).

module_quiz.question: @Autowired does?
module_quiz.explanation: Spring automatically finds and injects the matching bean (implementation) for the interface.

module_quiz.question: Single Responsibility means?
options: Each class has ONE reason to change
module_quiz.explanation: A class should have only one job. UserService handles users, EmailService handles emails.

module_quiz.question: Dependency Inversion means?
options: Depend on abstractions not concrete classes
module_quiz.explanation: Code against interfaces. OrderService depends on Database interface, not MySQLDatabase class.

module_quiz.question: JpaRepository provides?
module_quiz.explanation: findAll, findById, save, delete — all generated automatically from method names.

quiz.question: String is primitive?
quiz.explanation: String is a class. 8 primitives: byte short int long float double char boolean

quiz.question: final variable means?
quiz.explanation: final = constant after initialization

quiz.question: Extend how many classes?
quiz.explanation: Single inheritance. Multiple interfaces allowed.

quiz.question: == for strings checks?
quiz.explanation: == checks memory reference. Use .equals() for content.

quiz.question: ArrayList.get(i) complexity?
quiz.explanation: Direct array index access.

quiz.question: HashSet allows duplicates?
quiz.explanation: Set = unique elements only.

quiz.question: HashMap key must be?
quiz.explanation: Keys need consistent equals and hashCode.

quiz.question: abstract class can have constructors?
quiz.explanation: Abstract classes have constructors called by subclasses via super().

quiz.question: try-with-resources does?
quiz.explanation: Resources implementing AutoCloseable auto-close.

quiz.question: main method signature?
quiz.explanation: Exact signature required for JVM entry point.

quiz.question: StringBuilder vs String in loop?
quiz.explanation: String creates new objects each concat. StringBuilder modifies in place.

quiz.question: Checked vs unchecked exception?
options: Checked must be handled unchecked optional
quiz.explanation: Checked: IOException. Unchecked: NullPointerException. Compiler enforces checked.

quiz.question: Lambda (a,b) -> a+b is?
quiz.explanation: Concise syntax for single-method interface implementation.

quiz.question: stream().filter().collect() — filter is?
quiz.explanation: Intermediate ops are lazy until terminal op called.

quiz.question: Optional prevents?
quiz.explanation: Forces explicit handling of absent values.

quiz.question: synchronized keyword does?
options: Only one thread enters at a time
quiz.explanation: Mutual exclusion — prevents race conditions.

quiz.question: ConcurrentHashMap vs HashMap?
quiz.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap uses segment locking for thread-safe concurrent access.

quiz.question: Runnable vs Callable?
quiz.explanation: Callable.call() returns result and can throw checked exceptions.

quiz.question: Collections.unmodifiableList does?
quiz.explanation: Returns wrapper that throws UnsupportedOperationException on modification.

quiz.question: String pool is?
quiz.explanation: JVM caches string literals for reuse. Same literal = same object.

quiz.question: Override equals must also override?
quiz.explanation: hashCode contract: equal objects must have equal hash codes.

quiz.question: Java Record is?
options: Immutable data class with auto methods
quiz.explanation: Auto-generates constructor getters equals hashCode toString.

quiz.question: Best Singleton?
quiz.explanation: Enum handles serialization reflection thread-safety automatically.

quiz.question: PECS means?
quiz.explanation: Read = extends. Write = super. Generic wildcard rule.

quiz.question: Predicate functional interface returns?
quiz.explanation: Predicate.test(T) returns boolean for filtering.

quiz.question: Builder pattern for?
options: Complex objects with many optional params
quiz.explanation: Readable construction with method chaining.

quiz.question: Observer pattern?
options: Multiple subscribers notified on state change
quiz.explanation: Publish-subscribe event notification.

quiz.question: Local variable stored on?
quiz.explanation: Local variables and method frames on stack. Objects on heap.

quiz.question: System.gc() guarantees?
quiz.explanation: JVM can ignore gc() request entirely.

quiz.question: Static inner class vs inner class?
options: Static has no outer reference preventing leaks
quiz.explanation: Non-static inner holds implicit outer reference causing potential leaks.

quiz.question: @Autowired does?
quiz.explanation: Spring finds and injects matching bean.

quiz.question: Single Responsibility?
options: Each class ONE reason to change
quiz.explanation: One job per class.

quiz.question: Dependency Inversion?
quiz.explanation: Code against interfaces not implementations.

quiz.question: CompletableFuture for?
quiz.explanation: Non-blocking async with thenApply thenCompose chaining.

quiz.question: volatile keyword?
options: Variable always read from main memory not cache
quiz.explanation: Prevents thread caching. All threads see latest value.

quiz.question: Stream.parallel() does?
quiz.explanation: Splits stream into sub-streams processed by ForkJoinPool threads.

quiz.question: JpaRepository provides?
quiz.explanation: Auto-generates findAll save delete findById from method names.

quiz.question: Diamond problem solved by?
options: Interfaces with default methods + explicit override
quiz.explanation: If two interfaces have same default method, implementing class must override explicitly.

quiz.question: G1GC is?
quiz.explanation: G1 (Garbage First) balances throughput and latency. Default since Java 9.

quiz.question: @FunctionalInterface has?
quiz.explanation: Functional interface = exactly one abstract method. Can have default and static methods.
